speaker: interviewer
text: You're having to prove to the Hungarians that you belong there after the war began.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But I wanted to say something about my family first. You know, I told you that my family came to this town in 1709. And as a matter of fact, my grandfather-- there are many things that I only know from hearsay. My grandfather, at one point, was the mayor of this town. Not in my time, though. And the Jews were very much integrated, meaning that there was not much anti-Semitism. They were part of the town-- going to school there and so on. Well, I had an uncle. My father was an only child because this uncle died. And as a matter of fact, his mother died when she was three-- when he was three. I had an uncle who went to the university in Vienna. And usually those people became irreligious, going to university. My uncle went to university and yeshiva, both. And he was the tutor at the Rothschild's household in Vienna. I don't know for how long. I just know that he was 21 when he died of TB.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? His name was Shabtai. Shabtai Fuchs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was the tutor there for several years. And he went to the yeshiva, and he was studying with them in Hebrew also. Because, you know, others-- like I had two great uncles who also went to university. Can it be stopped while I'm coughing? Maybe a drink. I had two great uncles who went to university. But they-- they became irreligious. Later on, I knew them. They were completely irreligious. But my uncle was not. Then at age 21, he died of TB, and the Rothschilds sent back all his effects. I remember growing up with a list, all the things they sent back-- the books and everything. And I told you that my father was-- well, I don't know if I told you that he was quite brilliant, but he was. He had a very good memory, and he was especially good in history. And when he was here visiting, he had a-- he had a competition going on with his grandson, with Bruce.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was learning ancient history, and my father, of course, who knows when he read it? And they had a competition, who will catch whom not knowing something. Well, apparently Bruce could not catch him at anything. He knew everything that he learned right then in the summer in high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very good at that. I remember history especially-- whatever he was interested in. But it's quite a shocker to me. I always knew through my childhood, I mean, anybody criticizing my father-- and there were many people criticizing my father in the family-- but they said, but he is very learned and brilliant. You know, there was always a but.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I assumed he went to school and he learned all this. I mean, I didn't know. So once I asked him. When he was here, I asked him, tati, how many years did you go to school? He says, I never went to school. I says, what do you mean? He says, I went to school one day, and I didn't like it. So I didn't-- I didn't go-- I didn't go anymore. So I said, then where did you learn, I mean, after all, all the things you know? So he told me that when all the books were sent back-- his brother's books-- he read them all. And he had a very good memory, so he knew them all. So I said, but that must've been in German. How did you know German? He says, big deal. So I learned German, he said. So I said, but how could you remember all this? He said, eh, that's nothing to it. Just like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he never went to school. Of course, the yeshivas, he went. And he had [NON-ENGLISH]. But public school, he never went. Whatever he learned was from those books that my uncle had sent back, or they sent back which belonged to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as far as-- oh, I mentioned the fact that, all of a sudden, in 1941, 1940, the Jews had to prove that they belonged in Hungary. Well, apparently, it wasn't too much problem for my family because I don't remember having to be worried or anything. And my family in general, my grandparents also, I suppose they were able to prove that easily.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were many people who could not. I mean, they might have come like a generation before. That wasn't enough. Or they might have come, like, we had some new people in town. I don't know where they came from. They were not able to prove it. Even though they lived there and were born there and everything else, they couldn't prove it. And they deported those Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- they deported them in 1941 to-- they put them over the border of Poland. And then we heard later that they were massacred by the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were actually several ways. First of all, after the war, I heard. But rumors were rampant, even in those days. I mean, all of a sudden, they take those people away. We don't know where they go or anything at all. And of course, we try to, I suppose, save our sanity by saying, well, these people didn't belong here. The fact that people can just be taken and deported, I suppose we accepted that. Accepted that. I mean, we think of it now, think back, it's such a horrifying thing to just simply say, you don't belong here. Here you go. Put in cars, cattle cars, or I don't know what kind-- railway-- railroads cars-- and ship them off. Well, then they started asking more, and wanting to send away people who weren't as temporary as, as these. But it was in 1941 where even my grandparents were told that they are going to be taken away. And then the-- somebody or other just changed their minds and the whole thing stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandparents still lived in that small village. What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They, as a matter of fact, just wanted to empty the whole village of Jews, 15 Jews. I mean four years-- three years before they actually did do that. But things were very precarious, really, because-- but little by little you start accepting these things. I mean, what a horrifying thing to, to hear announced, from now on, Jews cannot have any businesses. How horrifying that really is. But little by little, they got us used to hearing those terrible words. Or Jews cannot own radios. Jews didn't own radios. And you know, you just kind of adjusted, switched gears, and went on. And we did not know what was really happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: To you-- what happened to you after the war began?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to us, not much happened, really, because we didn't have any business. And my father was buying and selling. By that time, my brother-- let's see. in '41, he was 19. He was born in '22. He was 19, and he, more or less, took over the support of the family. He started supporting the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: By doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, in 1939, he was sent-- he was on yeshiva. And he came home because it was rather difficult to be anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he at? Which school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which school? Which yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nitra. Somewhere in-- they called it Oberland, further west.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia. And then, when Czechoslovakia was dismembered, it was just taken away. So he came home. And at that point, he really took over the support of the family. My father stopped teaching. My father stopped anything, everything. He just did everything, meaning he would buy things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, the only thing you could do is black marketeering. What does black marketeering mean? It means that you're not allowed to slaughter animals anymore, so people did it in the-- in the basement. So my brother would buy a calf and bring the shochet and slaughter, and, and he knew all about. I mean, he taught himself how to take care of it and sold the meat. You sold the meat to Jewish-- who did you sell it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Jewish people, but there was also non-kosher meat, which they sold to Hungarians. But no one really told the authorities. So little by little, we are used to not be able to do this and not being able to do that. We just accepted it, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, particularly, yourself, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was finished with elementary school. And, of course, I had to do something because, first of all, my family needed help, and also a higher education was not available to Jews. So I had to learn a trade. And I hated the idea of having to learn a trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally I said, OK. And nothing went well. I wanted to be a photographer, by the way, competition for you. I would have become, but you had to-- you had to work Shabbat, which couldn't be. I even want to be a furrier. That didn't go. Also Shabbat, and what else? A hat maker. So I had to be a seamstress. So I said, if I'll be a seamstress, it has to be the best. So there was one in Khust, a neighboring town, who was, I would say, the equivalent of Christian Dior. I mean, really. And I went to apply. Couldn't get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, I was accepted. And you had to study for three years, in order to-- and that without cutting, without the-- just the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what work?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave you the nothing work, you know, to, to do finishing job. That's all. I mean, it was slave labor. That's what it really was. Because she got free work from the girls, which I was delighted to do because in three years, I'll be a Christian Dior. I never finished because, of course, 1944 came along. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You traveled back and forth to Khust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I stayed with my-- well, I had family in Khust. And I'm sure you heard about kest for yeshiva boys. You know what that means?

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, kest is when-- boys went to the yeshiva in a different town. They didn't go with enough money to go to a restaurant and eat, and they certainly didn't have yeshivas where they had kitchens and were able to feed the boys. So the townspeople would obligate themselves to feed a boy once a week, let's say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So each boy would go to seven different families for the week's food, three times a day. That was kest, which means board, really. Well, I was going to a different town, but I have family there. That was never-- girls never did any such thing. Well, I ate kest, meaning I ate-- family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two uncles and two great uncles in that town, and they were all well off, and yet would not feed me seven days a week. It was difficult, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the war begun by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But they all were quite well off, but still, people didn't obligate themselves to do something like that. I had two great uncles, and I had the two uncles, and my mother had some cousins. So I ate two days at one uncle's, two days at an aunt's, and one day at a great uncle, one day at another great uncle, and one day at this cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life seemed to go on naturally during this war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is what I'm saying, that people just adjusted. They did not know what really was happening because you were not allowed to have radios. You were not allowed to have newspapers, and the newspapers were censored anyway. We did see newspapers, Hungarian. And you didn't-- they didn't tell you what was really happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So no, we did not know. We were most-- so ignorant. Ignorance really killed us. It's the ignorance that did it. Also the Germans must have learned their lesson, because-- I mean, afterwards, I read about all those killings at the site of graves that the victims dug themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And things were too bloody, and the population around it, they were happy to see it, but still, rumors might have spread. So they wanted to stop that. So they started becoming smart for public relations. They made sure that you didn't know anything. They did not print anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any time-- like, for instance, even when they took these people in 1941, where are they taking them? To Poland, to a camp to work. They'll work there. They'll be happy there. They did not tell the truth. And we were stupid enough to believe what they said. So I was in that town trying to learn how to sew. I don't know a thing about sewing. I can tell you that much. I feel the only thing I learned at this place is to discern what is a good dress and what isn't. It's was to my disadvantage. Because any dress which isn't perfect, I will-- I can tell. But no, I didn't learn anything. But was there a social life for the Jewish boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young for this to matter. Besides, I go with a boy? It was something terrible. I remember there were boys interested, and I said, no, no. What would my aunt and uncle say? No, couldn't. We didn't do some of those things. I remember I once let a boy take me home. I said, but you have to go three blocks away before I get home because I won't be seen. No, there wasn't really much going-- yeah, have you ever heard of a [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a [NON-ENGLISH]? Well, a [NON-ENGLISH] is on Shabbat afternoon and evening. Boys and girls-- there's the main street, you know, like the important business street where all the businesses are closed on Shabbat. Boys and girls went back and forth and back and forth, and eyeing each other, and seeing what clothes they wear, and gossiping. So there is where I saw boys, but I didn't talk to them. No. I didn't have a social life. And there was no groups that you belonged to? No--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They did not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Zionist groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, some years before, when I was younger, there was somebody who was in Prague from our town who was in Betar. And he came into our town back home, and he started a group of Betar Youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Betar Youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar is a Zionist non-religious group. So of course, they didn't like that. The town elders didn't like that, but we went anyway. And they taught us some Hebrew. Like I had three lessons of Hebrew. But aside from that, which was a few years before, in 1944, no, there was nothing. Besides, the older boys and girls-- no, they didn't have anything else going, maybe they had something. No, they didn't. They really didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Women-- my mother never went to synagogue. That's why I never go. Except Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. When people ask me, what synagogue do you belong to? I usually say, you mean what synagogue do I go three times a year to? I guess I got into the habit of not going on a regular basis. No, women didn't go to the synagogue, but men did, like every day. No, I don't know of any groups. Anyway, then in 1944, on Purim, the Germans took over the Hungarian-- Hungary, as a matter of fact. Well, many of these things I know from reading about it after the war. A lot was going on I didn't know. I just knew that, all of a sudden, the Hungarians are there. And then on Purim, the Germans come in. But it's only after the war that I found out that Horthy-- which was the head of the Hungarian government-- began balking at some of the things. So the Germans simply took over. Didn't want to take chances.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on Purim, they took over. And I suppose we knew enough to know to be afraid of Germans. So I immediately went home to be with my family. But the Germans, were going around saying, oh, they're not bad. What are they talking about? They're fine. They're wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: They behaved so well. Didn't do anything whatsoever. Did not promulgate any new loss, even. Nothing. They were there from Purim to Pesach. And during Pesach, we were told that we are going to be taken away after Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in retrospect, I realized what really was happening. They were very anxious not to alarm the population. So they weren't going to say anything before our Pesach. Who ever is going to go away from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, they learned about the Jewish religion sufficiently and psychology. And they realized that they are going to have a rebellion on their hands, that people will start running away, and yelling, and doing. That's not an easy thing to handle. Because there were only about 125 families in my town, and we were surrounded by forests and mountains. If they would alarm us, we'd do at least something. No, nothing at all. They walked. They did not misbehave in any way whatsoever, and did not tell us anything till after Pesach. And this I see as simply studying everything about the Jews and knowing what they're going to do. This-- I don't know if we realized this, that this was not just a random thing. In the beginning, it was random. I mean, the plan was the same. But they worked randomly, meaning they would gather as many Jews as they could and kill them immediately. At this point, they wanted to simply finish their job as easily and as quietly as they can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finish their job meant what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of killing the Jews, each and every one of them. They had a plan, and they went about it in the most efficient way possible. And they, they knew what the efficient way was. And we were stupid enough not to know. We were simply ignorant and did not know what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't there any discussions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz, we certainly never heard of. Never. Extermination camps, we never heard of, meaning we didn't even have rumors. Some rumors, we had about those people who were taken in 1941. And there, we did have rumors, and even facts, but people were afraid to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like some people came back. Well, those people couldn't go back to their town, because, I mean, their houses were taken over, and so on. So they had to go to different towns, and change their names, and not confess that they're the ones who were taken away from their place. Because if they'd confess, they'd come and take them again, and who knows what would happen? So we didn't know. There are some people kind of don't-- not in our town, but in Khust, where I was at that time-- there are some people kind of don't belong, but we didn't know what's what. And you mustn't talk about it because it might be dangerous for them. So when they, the Germans, came in 1944 on Purim, they came with a plan. And I'm sure Eichmann was involved with that because supposedly he learned quite a bit about Jews and the Jewish religion. Even to the extent where we said, oh, there's nothing wrong with the Germans. So you were still celebrating Purim, you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Purim and Pesach like usual, baked matzahs, everything, fine. Two days-- the day after Pesach they told us you'll have to be gathered. Two days after, we were all put into-- we had a big synagogue which, by the way, now, when I went back, I saw they made it into a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the population told me it was a Jew who came back, and he started it, so like they got permission to do it. It was a very beautiful synagogue. All the Jews were gathered there, and we were told to take whatever-- I don't remember how many kilos. How much can you carry?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they're going to resettle us because we heard the Russians were approaching out town. We heard bombardments. We must have been quite close because within a few weeks after they took us away, the Russians were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vysk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In all these towns. So they very quietly, and we very quietly went to the synagogue because they're going to-- after all, we knew we weren't loyal citizens to the Germans. We knew that. And they are smart, too, we figured. They are not going to leave us here where the Russians are coming, and we can betray them. In other words, it even made sense. It even made sense to have them take us away. So we went because this is war, and the Germans don't want to be betrayed. So we went quietly and nicely to the synagogue, all of us on the floor, sitting there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone willingly? Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother didn't want to, actually. He said to my parents, look, why should we schlep the children? He knew the mountains like the back of his hand because he was dealing with all those people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, I'll take the smaller children, and I'm going to-- and then when you'll come back, I'll bring them back. My mother said, my children? I'm not going to let them go. What do you mean? What are they going to do, poor kids? How old were the younger ones, by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest was five. The next one was nine, and the next one was almost 13.
